name: Horn of Africa Drought
region: Southern Somalia, Southern Ethiopia, Eastern Kenya
headline: Five failed rainy seasons. 43,000 dead in Somalia alone. This drought could not have happened without climate change.
justice_framing: Somalia, Ethiopia and Kenya together contributed 1.4% of global cumulative emissions. The drought that killed 43,000 people in Somalia alone could not have happened without climate change caused elsewhere. The humanitarian appeal to respond to it was funded at roughly 40% of what was requested.

quote.text: What we found is that climate change has made the drought exceptional. These conditions are likely to continue in the future as the climate continues to warm.
quote.attribution: Joyce Kimutai, Climate Scientist, Kenya Meteorological Department · WWA study, April 2023

attribution.likelihood_ratio_note: Conservative estimate. The WWA team found the drought would not have occurred at all in a 1.2°C cooler world – the 100x figure understates the true climate signal.
attribution.method: Observational trend analysis and climate model ensemble – 24-month precipitation and evapotranspiration over southern Somalia, southern Ethiopia, eastern Kenya, January 2021 to December 2022
attribution.key_mechanism: Increased evapotranspiration from higher temperatures was the primary driver – not just reduced rainfall. A warmer atmosphere draws more moisture from soil and plants, intensifying drought even when rainfall is only slightly below average.
attribution.summary: This drought would not have happened in a world without human-caused climate change. The combined effect of reduced rainfall and dramatically increased evapotranspiration – driven by higher temperatures – made the drought at least 100 times more likely. Five consecutive failed rainy seasons between 2020 and 2022 were driven primarily by the temperature-driven increase in moisture loss from soils and plants.

method_explainer.what_attribution_science_is: For droughts, attribution science examines two things together: rainfall deficits and evapotranspiration – the rate at which moisture is lost from soils and plants due to heat. A warmer world loses more moisture even when rainfall is similar, making droughts more severe. Scientists compare these combined conditions across climate models with and without human greenhouse gas emissions.
method_explainer.how_this_event_was_studied: WWA scientists analysed a 24-month period from January 2021 to December 2022 across the Southern Horn of Africa. They examined both seasons separately – the March-May long rains and the October-December short rains – and combined. They used observational rainfall records and 19 climate models from Kenya, Ethiopia, Somalia, the Netherlands, Germany, the UK, and the US. The strongest climate signal was in evapotranspiration, not rainfall.
method_explainer.what_confidence_means: High confidence because the temperature signal driving evapotranspiration is one of the most robust findings in climate science. The 100x figure is explicitly described as a conservative lower bound – the actual increase in likelihood is likely higher but the models could not reliably estimate beyond that threshold.

human_impact.people_affected_note: 36.4 million in need of emergency assistance across Ethiopia, Somalia and Kenya per OCHA 2023.
human_impact.deaths_note: 43,000 estimated deaths in Somalia alone in 2022, of which approximately half were children under five years old. Based on excess mortality analysis by LSHTM researchers. True toll across the region is higher and unknown.
human_impact.deaths_source: London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine excess mortality study, cited by Imperial College / WHO
human_impact.displaced_note: 3.3 million displaced across Kenya, Ethiopia and Somalia per UNHCR. At least 180,000 refugees crossed into drought-stricken areas from Somalia and South Sudan.
human_impact.economic_loss_note: No consolidated economic loss figure. Livestock losses, crop failures, and long-term food insecurity impacts are severe but not aggregated into a single assessment.
human_impact.malnutrition_note: 942,000 children aged 6-59 months with acute malnutrition in Somalia alone as of December 2022.
human_impact.emitters_share_note: East Africa's share of global cumulative greenhouse gas emissions – the starkest emissions-to-impact injustice ratio of any event in this dataset.
human_impact.emitters_share_source: Carbon Brief analysis / Our World in Data

satellite.description: CHIRPS rainfall anomaly showing deviation from 1981-2010 baseline across the Horn of Africa study region

at_risk.context: Across Ethiopia, Kenya and Somalia, pastoralist communities whose entire livelihoods depend on livestock watched their herds die as pasture disappeared and water sources dried up. Livestock are both income and food security – losing them means losing everything.

In Somalia alone, 942,000 children aged 6-59 months were acutely malnourished by December 2022. Half of the estimated 43,000 drought deaths were children under five. Malnutrition at this age causes permanent developmental harm.

Somalia's ongoing conflict made aid delivery dangerous and inconsistent. State fragility dramatically amplified the climate impact – as the WWA study notes, climate change determines hazard severity, but vulnerability determines human cost.

future_outlook.label: Horn of Africa · current emissions trajectory

stats.label: already more likely than pre-industrial era
stats.delta: and rising with every fraction of a degree of warming

stats.label: more likely for long rains to fail
stats.delta: due to climate change in the March-May season specifically

stats.label: of global emissions from East Africa
stats.delta: bearing the consequences of decisions made elsewhere

response.newsapi_query: Horn of Africa drought Somalia famine

key_outcomes.description: The UN Flash Appeal for the Horn of Africa drought was funded at roughly 40% of the requested amount throughout 2022 and 2023. In humanitarian terms, that gap is the difference between survival and starvation for specific, countable numbers of people. International attention consistently lagged behind need despite the scale being documented in real time.
key_outcomes.status_note: As of 2024, recovery remains incomplete. Recurrent drought seasons compound losses that communities have not recovered from.

key_outcomes.description: The Horn of Africa drought was prominently cited by advocates for the Loss and Damage Fund at COP27 in November 2022. The fund that Horn of Africa advocates helped create has since received pledges that would not cover one month of Somalia's drought losses. Joyce Kimutai and the Kenyan Meteorological Department researchers who authored the WWA attribution study are themselves from the affected region – the scientists proving the injustice live inside it.
key_outcomes.status_note: For the first time, the scientific case is strong enough that climate liability for these specific events is legally arguable. That is a historic shift even if the institutional response remains inadequate.

response.accountability_summary: The humanitarian response reached millions but the structural causes – emissions from wealthy nations, chronic underfunding of adaptation – remain unaddressed.

curated_coverage.headline: Global warming made Horn of Africa drought possible – and 100 times more likely
Deadly drought in Horn of Africa would not have happened without climate change
43,000 estimated dead in Somalia drought – half of them children under five
Five failed rains: how climate change turned drought into famine across the Horn
